entities.name: varṇa
entities.cite: The 'a' in one word and in another is not two similar sounds but, as competent speakers unhesitatingly judge, the same phoneme

entities.name: pada
entities.cite: the word 'gauh' in two different sentences is one word, not twins

entities.name: ekatva
entities.cite: This identity-across-occurrence is the most elementary fact a science of language must save

entities.name: dhvani
entities.cite: the acoustic events are numerically distinct and unrepeatable

axioms.name: ekatva
axioms.cite: The 'a' in one word and in another is not two similar sounds but, as competent speakers unhesitatingly judge, the same phoneme

axioms.name: ekatva
axioms.cite: the word 'gauh' in two different sentences is one word, not twins

axioms.name: vivartate
axioms.cite: Whatever phonemes and words are, they are repeatables — the type, not the dated token, is the grammarian's object

claims.name: ekatva
claims.cite: the unity of the phonemes is not abolished

claims.name: ekatva
claims.cite: one and the same word is found

rejected_readings.label: token_physicalism
rejected_readings.rendering: Acoustic events are the true phonemes and words; different utterances produce different phonemic and word units.
rejected_readings.why: Commits to crude token-physicalism, which the verse rejects. Phonemes and words are types (repeatables), not acoustic tokens. The numerical distinctness of acoustic events does not entail phonemic distinctness.

rejected_readings.label: similarity_not_sameness
rejected_readings.rendering: Phonemes in different words are merely similar to one another; words in different sentences are merely similar.
rejected_readings.why: Reduces the verse's core claim—identity recognized by competent speakers—to mere similarity. The entire doctrine depends on the 'a' being the same phoneme across contexts, not different-but-similar ones.

notes: The contract captures the verse's foundational argument: phonemes (varṇa) and words (pada) are linguistic types with the property ekatva (identity-across-occurrence), manifesting as numerically distinct acoustic events without losing their unity. This blocks crude token-physicalism and establishes that the grammarian's object is the repeatable type, not the dated token—essential scaffolding for the parts-and-wholes argument in later verses.
